Inclusion Gate 3 = Evidence strong enough for near universal consensus

Exclusions to protect credibility

Exclude = Accidental harms like bias or negligence

Exclude = Dual use tools with unclear intent

Exclude = Grey zone pricing tactics unless explicitly exploitative and documented

column1: Gate 1 Met? = Intentionally designed, marketed, or deployed for harm (Y/N)
Gate 2 Met? = Harm is the product, not a side effect (Y/N)
Gate 3 Met? = Evidence strong enough for near universal consensus (Y/N)
Exclusion 1 Triggered? = Accidental harm only (Y/N)
Exclusion 2 Triggered? = Dual use with unclear intent (Y/N)
Exclusion 3 Triggered? = Grey zone only, not explicitly exploitative and documented (Y/N)
Include in Repo? = Y only if Gates 1 to 3 are Y and Exclusions 1 to 3 are N
1 Sentence Evidence Summary
column3: FraudGPT is an AI chatbot advertised on dark-web forums and Telegram that generates phishing emails, malware, and scam content for cybercriminals
column4: ICE uses a facial recognition application called Mobile Fortify that captures facial images and compares them with biometric databases to identify individuals in the field.
column5: Clearview AI built a facial recognition database of tens of billions of images scraped from the internet and allows law enforcement to identify people by uploading a photo.
column6: WormGPT is openly marketed as a hacking AI tool with no limits and has been widely documented as a cybercrime assistant.
column7: Security researchers report that GhostGPT is sold as an uncensored chatbot for malware creation, phishing, and exploit development.
column8: Evil-GPT has been advertised on dark web forums as an uncensored alternative to WormGPT for phishing and malware tasks.
column9: Threat researchers describe Xanthorox as a subscription black-hat AI platform marketed for cybercrime and malicious code generation.
column10: Investigations and lawsuits describe ClothOff as a site that turns clothed photos into fake nude images and has been used in harassment cases involving minors.
column11: PredPol was sold as a crime-forecasting system, but critics and later analyses tied it to ineffective predictions and the amplification of biased policing patterns.
column12: ShotSpotter is marketed as a gunshot-detection public safety tool, but critics say it is disproportionately deployed in Black neighborhoods and can intensify police encounters after false alerts.
column13: BriefCam markets AI video analytics for security and law enforcement, while courts and digital-rights groups have challenged some deployments as unlawful or excessively intrusive.
column14: Oosto markets live watchlist alerting and access control, while the company's earlier AnyVision deployments drew scrutiny over surveillance uses and facial recognition in sensitive contexts.
column15: Facewatch markets facial recognition to retailers as a crime-prevention tool, while privacy groups argue that scanning all shoppers is disproportionate and risks misidentification.
column16: San Francisco’s amended complaint identifies Ainude.ai as part of an undressing-site ecosystem that creates AI-generated nonconsensual intimate imagery and alleges knowing facilitation of that harm.
column17: Unit 42 and follow-on reporting describe KawaiiGPT as a purpose-built malicious LLM marketed for phishing, ransomware notes, and offensive scripting.
column18: Reporting describes SpamGPT as a purpose-built phishing toolkit that helps attackers generate scam email content and scale mass-mailing campaigns.
column19: Resecurity and follow-on reporting describe DIG AI as an uncensored darknet assistant explicitly marketed to criminals and terrorists for malicious outputs.
column20: Researchers reported that GXC Team sold Business Invoice Swapper specifically to hijack invoice threads and reroute payments to attacker-controlled accounts.
column21: Multiple reports describe LoveGPT as a scam tool that automates fake profiles and persuasive chats across major dating platforms.
column22: DOJ and investigative reporting document OnlyFake as a commercial service that sold more than 10,000 realistic fake IDs for fraud use.
column23: Security reporting identifies a dark-web tool sold as DarkBERT for malicious code, phishing pages, and underground cybercrime workflows.
column24: Fraud researchers describe DarkWizardAI as an underground multilingual fraud assistant marketed as a successor to earlier criminal LLMs.
column25: Google documented FRUITSHELL as malware using hard-coded prompts to bypass AI-driven security and support reverse-shell operations.
column26: Google and follow-on coverage describe PROMPTFLUX as AI-enabled malware that queries Gemini to rewrite itself for evasion and persistence.
column27: Google and SentinelOne describe QUIETVAULT as AI-enabled stealer malware that uses prompts to search victim systems and exfiltrate valuable data.
column28: GTIG reported HONESTCUE as new AI-integrated malware that uses Gemini to generate code for follow-on compromise stages.
column29: GTIG linked COINBAIT to an AI-generated phishing kit built with Lovable AI and used to impersonate a crypto exchange for credential harvesting.
column30: Multiple security firms describe LAMEHUG as a malware family that offloads command generation to a remote LLM during execution.
column31: SentinelOne and follow-on reporting describe MalTerminal as GPT-4-powered malware capable of generating malicious logic in real time.
column32: Google and SentinelOne reporting identify PromptLock as AI-enabled ransomware that dynamically generates parts of its malicious workflow.
column33: A joint cyber advisory and DOJ action describe Meliorator as covert AI-enhanced software used to generate fake personas and spread Russian disinformation.
column34: NewsGuard reported that the Pravda network published propaganda at industrial scale and poisoned leading AI chatbots with repeated false narratives.
column35: AP and CyberScoop reported that REST Media spread AI-driven disinformation in Moldova and was treated by researchers as a Russian cutout operation.
column36: DOJ and Cyber Command describe Doppelganger as a Russian disinformation campaign that used generative AI and spoofed domains to mimic trusted outlets.
column37: Recorded Future and ISD describe Operation Overload as a Russia-aligned campaign using fake news resources and AI-generated audio to manipulate public opinion.
column38: Microsoft reported that Storm-1516 used AI-enhanced deepfakes, fake personas, and multilingual disinformation to target elections and public opinion.
column39: Investigations reported that Habsora, or The Gospel, was used to rapidly generate bombing targets, raising concerns about civilian harm and scale of strikes.
column40: Investigative reporting described Lavender as an AI-powered target database used to identify tens of thousands of people for strikes with limited human review.
column41: Reporting states that Where's Daddy? was used to track suspected militants to their homes so attacks could be timed when they were present.
column42: Where’s Daddy is designed to make killings of Hamas agents easier through targeting them specifically when they’re at their house.
column43: Habsora, or the Gospel, is a fully autonomous AI that uses Israeli government and military data to identify potential targets.
column44: Pravda Network utilizes its own generative AI to flood Western audiences with disinformation that promotes Russian perspectives, especially regarding the Ukraine war.
column45: Clearview AI scraped billions of online photos to create a facial recognition database used by law enforcement agencies.
column46: PredPol analyzes historical crime data to predict crime locations, but critics argue it reinforces biased policing patterns.
column47: The system aggregates behavioral, financial, and legal data to produce trust scores that can affect travel, loans, and services.
column48: DarkBERT is an AI model trained on dark web data including hacker forums and illicit marketplaces to model and potentially enhance cybercriminal activities.

column83: Distinct from WormGPT and Evil-GPT; specifically emphasizes cryptographic malware and code obfuscation as its core differentiators. Marketing was explicit about criminal use. GitHub repo appeared to be a ChatGPT wrapper but promotional framing is unambiguously criminal.
column84: Distinct from DarkBERT (already listed). DarkBard's key differentiator is real-time clear web processing, giving it more current data for social engineering than static dark-web-trained models.
column85: Actual technical capabilities are limited (it is Auto-GPT with a modified goal prompt), but the deliberate, public design intent to cause human harm is unambiguous and widely documented. Unique case: the harm design is explicit rather than inferred.
column86: AI-generated content is the core product feature — not a side effect — enabling spam to bypass filters. OpenAI disabled the API key after SentinelOne's disclosure. Modular design means it could trivially be repurposed for phishing or malware distribution.
column87: Distinct from other malware on list because the AI feature (OCR for crypto seed phrases) is a designed, marketed product capability — not incidental use. Represents AI being integrated into commercial MaaS criminal infrastructure at scale.

column4: An AI system whose objective function, deployment context, and incentive alignment are structured to exploit vulnerability, remove moral agency, or systematically amplify power asymmetries in high-stakes domains.
column5: AI system that is intentionally designed for malicious intents & undermines human autonomy, dignity, safety / well-being

column3: I. Information and Perception Manipulation: Automated Disinformation Systems, Synthetic Media Deception, and  Narrative Amplification Engines (Shiv)  |  II. Exploitation and Manipulation: Predatory Targeting Systems, Addiction Optimization Systems, and Financial Extraction Algorithms (Diya)  |  III. Surveillance and Control: Mass Surveillance Systems, Predictive Suppression Systems, and Social Scoring Mechanisms  (Aayush) |  IV. Cyber and Infrastructure Harm: Automated Cyberattack Tools,  Infrastructure Disruption Systems, and Autonomous Weaponization (Shaurya)  |   V. Institutional and Structural Manipulation: Metric Gaming Systems, Market Manipulation Systems, and Accountability Evasion Systems (Dileep)
column4:
Does this AI system have the potential to cause evil in the hands of the wrong person?

If you rewrote the system’s objective in plain English, would it describe exploitation, control, punishment, or extraction?

Does the system function by identifying and acting upon human weakness or structural disadvantage?

Does this AI replace meaningful human moral judgment in high-stakes decisions?

Does this system increase the ability of institutions to dominate marginalized groups?

Is the optimization goal intrinsically exploitative?
column5: 1) Malicious by Design AI: built specifically to cause harm to others                                              2) Weaponzied AI: built for neutral
